edges.description: Expansion in the sellar/suprasellar region compresses or injures nearby optic, hypothalamic, pituitary, and third-ventricle structures.
Orphanet records cerebral calcification as a very frequent phenotype in craniopharyngioma.
Orphanet records intracranial cystic lesion as a very frequent phenotype in craniopharyngioma.
Orphanet records enlarged pituitary gland as a very frequent phenotype in craniopharyngioma.
Orphanet records abnormal hypothalamus morphology as an obligate phenotype in craniopharyngioma.
Orphanet records bitemporal hemianopia as a frequent visual-field phenotype in craniopharyngioma.
Orphanet records papilledema as a frequent phenotype in craniopharyngioma.
Orphanet records nausea and vomiting as a frequent phenotype in craniopharyngioma.
Orphanet records obesity as a frequent hypothalamic/endocrine phenotype in craniopharyngioma.
Orphanet records pituitary hypothyroidism as a frequent endocrine phenotype in craniopharyngioma.
Orphanet records central adrenal insufficiency as a frequent endocrine phenotype in craniopharyngioma.
CTNNB1-mutant ACP cluster cells signal to adjacent tumor, glial, and immune compartments through paracrine pathways.
Inflammatory and invasive-front biology can intensify local tissue reaction around critical hypothalamic and optic structures.
BRAF V600E-driven MAPK activation leads to papillary craniopharyngioma tumor growth in the sellar/suprasellar region, producing optic, hypothalamic, and pituitary mass effect.

BRAF/MEK Inhibition for BRAF V600E Papillary Craniopharyngioma
edges.target: BRAF V600E MAPK PCP Tumor Signaling

nodes.description: ACP contains a reactive glial and immune microenvironment with inflammatory, interferon, cytokine, and MAPK/ERK signaling programs. These pathways shape invasive front behavior, cystic tumor biology, and potential sensitivity to MEK inhibition or other anti-inflammatory strategies.

evidence.reference_title: Tumour compartment transcriptomics demonstrates the activation of inflammatory and odontogenic programmes in human adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma and identifies the MAPK/ERK pathway as a novel therapeutic target.
evidence.snippet: We validate these results by immunostaining against immune cell markers, cytokine ELISA and proteome analysis in both solid tumour and cystic fluid from ACP patients.

evidence.reference_title: Tumour compartment transcriptomics demonstrates the activation of inflammatory and odontogenic programmes in human adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma and identifies the MAPK/ERK pathway as a novel therapeutic target.
evidence.snippet: We reveal that inhibiting the MAPK/ERK pathway with trametinib, a clinically approved MEK inhibitor, results in reduced proliferation and increased apoptosis in explant cultures of human and mouse ACP.

evidence.reference_title: Multi-omics analysis of adamantinomatous craniopharyngiomas reveals distinct molecular subgroups with prognostic and treatment response significance.
evidence.snippet: The ImA and ImB subgroups had activated inflammatory and interferon response pathways, with enhanced immune cell infiltration and more predominantly cystic tumors.

nodes.description: Abnormal hypothalamus morphology is recorded as an obligate phenotype in Orphanet.

evidence.reference_title: Craniopharyngioma
evidence.snippet: HP:0012286 | Abnormal hypothalamus morphology | Obligate (100%)

meta.association: Somatic BRAF V600E mutation in papillary craniopharyngioma

evidence.reference_title: BRAF-MEK Inhibition in Newly Diagnosed Papillary Craniopharyngiomas.
evidence.snippet: Genotyping has shown that more than 90% of papillary craniopharyngiomas carry BRAF V600E mutations

nodes.description: Papillary craniopharyngioma is driven in most cases by BRAF V600E, which activates MAPK/ERK signaling and creates a genotype-matched therapeutic vulnerability to combined BRAF and MEK inhibition.
nodes.id: BRAF V600E MAPK PCP Tumor Signaling

evidence.reference_title: BRAF-MEK Inhibition in Newly Diagnosed Papillary Craniopharyngiomas.
evidence.snippet: Genotyping has shown that more than 90% of papillary craniopharyngiomas carry BRAF V600E mutations

evidence.reference_title: Practical application of precision oncology in adult onset craniopharyngiomas.
evidence.snippet: Identification of specific molecular driver mutations in each type- BRAF V600E in papillary craniopharyngiomas (PCP) and CTNNB1 in adamantinomatous craniopharyngiomas (ACP) has resulted in a paradigm shift in the management of adult CPs.

nodes.description: Combined BRAF and MEK inhibition, such as vemurafenib plus cobimetinib or dabrafenib plus trametinib, is a genotype-matched treatment strategy for BRAF V600E papillary craniopharyngioma and may reduce the need for morbid local therapy.
nodes.id: BRAF/MEK Inhibition for BRAF V600E Papillary Craniopharyngioma

evidence.reference_title: BRAF-MEK Inhibition in Newly Diagnosed Papillary Craniopharyngiomas.
evidence.snippet: The median reduction in the volume of the tumor was 91% (range, 68 to 99).

evidence.reference_title: Update on Neoadjuvant and Adjuvant BRAF Inhibitors in Papillary Craniopharyngioma: A Systematic Review.
evidence.snippet: Treatment is shifting to a wider multidisciplinary management, where a key role is played by targeted therapies, to improve outcomes and quality of life for patients with BRAF-mutated craniopharyngiomas.

nodes.description: Binimetinib MEK inhibition is under phase 2 clinical investigation for pediatric and young-adult recurrent ACP, supported by ACP MAPK pathway biology but not yet established as standard care.

evidence.reference_title: Phase 2 Study of the MEK Inhibitor MEKTOVI® (Binimetinib) for the Treatment of Pediatric Adamantinomatous Craniopharyngioma
evidence.snippet: In this Phase II, the drug will be used to treat pediatric patients diagnosed with recurrent Adamantinomatous Craniopharyngioma including patients who have undergone surgery and/or radiation therapy.

evidence.reference_title: Tumour compartment transcriptomics demonstrates the activation of inflammatory and odontogenic programmes in human adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma and identifies the MAPK/ERK pathway as a novel therapeutic target.
evidence.snippet: We reveal that inhibiting the MAPK/ERK pathway with trametinib, a clinically approved MEK inhibitor, results in reduced proliferation and increased apoptosis in explant cultures of human and mouse ACP.

nodes.description: Bitemporal hemianopia is a frequent visual-field phenotype in craniopharyngioma.

evidence.reference_title: Craniopharyngioma
evidence.snippet: HP:0030521 | Bitemporal hemianopia | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Intracystic or local brachytherapy is an alternative radiation-based strategy for selected cystic craniopharyngiomas, with long-term progression-free-survival evidence from pooled clinical trials.

evidence.reference_title: Brachytherapy in craniopharyngiomas: a systematic review and meta-analysis of long-term follow-up.
evidence.snippet: Brachytherapy has been indicated as an alternative option for treating cystic craniopharyngiomas (CPs).

evidence.reference_title: Brachytherapy in craniopharyngiomas: a systematic review and meta-analysis of long-term follow-up.
evidence.snippet: The results of the meta-analysis showed that 1-year, 2-3 years and 5 years progression free survival rates (PFS) are 75% (95%CI: 66-84%), 62% (95%CI: 52-72%) and 57% (95%CI: 22-92%), respectively.

meta.association: Somatic activating mutation in adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma

evidence.reference_title: The molecular pathogenesis of craniopharyngiomas.
evidence.snippet: ACP and PCP subtypes can be identified by the presence of mutations in CTNNB1 and BRAF genes, with prevalence around 60% and 90%, respectively.

nodes.description: In adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma, somatic CTNNB1 alterations stabilize beta-catenin and activate Wnt signaling in tumor clusters. These clusters act as signaling centers that support epithelial proliferation, cystic/solid architecture, and paracrine interaction with surrounding glial and immune tissue.

evidence.reference_title: Tumour compartment transcriptomics demonstrates the activation of inflammatory and odontogenic programmes in human adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma and identifies the MAPK/ERK pathway as a novel therapeutic target.
evidence.snippet: Adamantinomatous craniopharyngiomas (ACPs) are clinically challenging tumours, the majority of which have activating mutations in CTNNB1.

evidence.reference_title: Multi-omics analysis of adamantinomatous craniopharyngiomas reveals distinct molecular subgroups with prognostic and treatment response significance.
evidence.snippet: The WNT subgroup showed higher Wnt/β-catenin pathway activity, with a greater number of epithelial cells and more predominantly solid tumors.

nodes.description: Central adrenal insufficiency is a frequent endocrine phenotype in craniopharyngioma.

evidence.reference_title: Craniopharyngioma
evidence.snippet: HP:0011734 | Central adrenal insufficiency | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: Craniopharyngioma
evidence.snippet: HP:0002514 | Cerebral calcification | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Enlarged pituitary gland is a very frequent structural phenotype in craniopharyngioma.

evidence.reference_title: Craniopharyngioma
evidence.snippet: HP:0012505 | Enlarged pituitary gland | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Intracranial cystic lesion is a very frequent imaging phenotype in craniopharyngioma.

evidence.reference_title: Craniopharyngioma
evidence.snippet: HP:0010576 | Intracranial cystic lesion | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Surgery is used to establish diagnosis, decompress symptomatic mass effect, and remove as much tumor as safely possible while limiting hypothalamic, optic, and pituitary injury.

evidence.reference_title: Craniopharyngioma: A comprehensive review of the clinical presentation, radiological findings, management, and future Perspective.
evidence.snippet: In almost all craniopharyngioma cases, surgery is indicated to: establish the diagnosis, relieve mass-related symptoms, and remove as much tumor as is safely possible.

nodes.description: Nausea and vomiting are frequent symptoms reported in craniopharyngioma.

evidence.reference_title: Craniopharyngioma
evidence.snippet: HP:0002017 | Nausea and vomiting | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Obesity is a frequent hypothalamic/endocrine phenotype in craniopharyngioma.

evidence.reference_title: Craniopharyngioma
evidence.snippet: HP:0001513 | Obesity | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Local tumor extension and treatment effects injure the optic chiasma, pituitary gland, hypothalamus, and adjacent ventricular pathways, explaining visual impairment, headache, hypopituitarism, diabetes insipidus, and hydrocephalus or raised intracranial pressure.

evidence.reference_title: Craniopharyngioma: A comprehensive review of the clinical presentation, radiological findings, management, and future Perspective.
evidence.snippet: Due to its unique anatomical locations, the most frequently reported clinical manifestations are headache, visual impairment, nausea/vomiting, and endocrine deficiencies resulting in sexual dysfunction in adults and growth failure in children.

nodes.description: Papilledema is a frequent ophthalmologic phenotype in craniopharyngioma.

evidence.reference_title: Craniopharyngioma
evidence.snippet: HP:0001085 | Papilledema | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Pituitary hypothyroidism is a frequent endocrine phenotype in craniopharyngioma.

evidence.reference_title: Craniopharyngioma
evidence.snippet: HP:0008245 | Pituitary hypothyroidism | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Radiation therapy is used with or after surgery for local disease control, but must be balanced against morbidity involving the optic, hypothalamic-pituitary, and adjacent brain structures.
nodes.id: Radiation Therapy for Local Tumor Control

evidence.reference_title: BRAF-MEK Inhibition in Newly Diagnosed Papillary Craniopharyngiomas.
evidence.snippet: Treatment with the use of surgery, radiation, or both is often associated with substantial morbidity related to vision loss, neuroendocrine dysfunction, and memory loss.

evidence.reference_title: Craniopharyngioma: A comprehensive review of the clinical presentation, radiological findings, management, and future Perspective.
evidence.snippet: Recent neurosurgical technical advances, including innovative surgical approaches, detailed radiotherapy protocols, targeted therapy, replacement of lost hormonal functions and quality of life all have the potential to improve the outcome of patients with craniopharyngioma.

nodes.description: Craniopharyngioma arises from epithelial remnants of Rathke pouch in the sellar/parasellar and suprasellar region, producing a slow-growing but space-occupying epithelial mass near the pituitary gland and hypothalamus.

evidence.reference_title: Craniopharyngioma: A comprehensive review of the clinical presentation, radiological findings, management, and future Perspective.
evidence.snippet: Craniopharyngioma (CP) is a rare intracranial tumor arising from the epithelial remnants of Rathke's pouch, most frequently originating in the sellar/parasellar region.
